color_profiles.name: classic-banded
color_profiles.cause: Alternating microporosity and impurity concentration in silica bands.

color_profiles.name: iron-banded
color_profiles.cause: Iron oxides (hematite, goethite, limonite) concentrated in alternating bands.

color_profiles.name: blue-lace-agate
color_profiles.cause: Scattering effects and trace impurities; very fine banding.

color_profiles.name: fire-agate
color_profiles.cause: Thin-film interference from layered iron oxide (goethite) inclusions within silica layers.

color_profiles.name: moss-agate
colors: colorless to white with green or brown moss-like inclusions
color_profiles.cause: Dendritic or filiform chlorite, hornblende, or manganese oxide inclusions.

color_profiles.name: onyx
color_profiles.cause: Straight parallel banding with black bands (carbon or manganese) and white bands.

minerals.visual_notes: Defining characteristic is banded or patterned appearance — concentric, plume, fortification, or other structured color patterns. The concentric fortification pattern in cross-section looks like aerial topography or castle walls.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Variable; some specimens show greenish or yellowish fluorescence under UV.
optical_effects.iridescence: Fire agate shows vivid iridescence from thin-film interference in iron oxide layers.
optical_effects.play_of_color: Fire agate shows vivid iridescence from thin-film interference in iron oxide layers.

color_profiles.name: Classic Alexandrite Color Change
colors: emerald-green to bluish-green in daylight; red to purplish-red in incandescent light
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ in BeAl2O4 orthorhombic field: dual transmission windows in red and blue-green of visible spectrum; daylight (blue-rich) emphasizes green transmission; incandescent (red-rich) emphasizes red transmission.

color_profiles.name: Brazilian Alexandrite
colors: brownish-green to brownish-red; less vivid than Russian
color_profiles.cause: Same Cr3+ mechanism; slight Fe contamination shifts color toward brown.

color_profiles.name: Cat's Eye Alexandrite
color_profiles.cause: Color change from Cr3+ combined with chatoyancy from parallel tube inclusions or needle inclusions.

minerals.visual_notes: Alexandrite is unique — a single stone with two identities. Under northern daylight or fluorescent light it is distinctly green; under incandescent (candle, tungsten) light it turns vivid red-purple. The best Russian specimens shift from 'emerald' to 'ruby' — an impossible color change nicknamed 'the emerald by day, ruby by night' gem. No other natural gemstone produces such a dramatic and complete color change.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Moderate red fluorescence under UV (Cr3+ emission, same mechanism as ruby)
optical_effects.chatoyancy: Cat's eye alexandrite — strong chatoyancy from oriented needles; combines color change + cat's eye
optical_effects.asterism: Extremely rare four-rayed star alexandrite documented

color_profiles.name: Baltic Golden / Honey Amber
color_profiles.cause: Oxidation and polymerization of original resin; iron oxide traces may deepen color.

color_profiles.name: Butterscotch / Opaque Amber
color_profiles.cause: Micro-bubbles of water and air trapped during resin flow; scattering makes amber opaque.

color_profiles.name: Dominican Blue / Green Amber
colors: blue-green when viewed in reflected light; golden in transmitted light
color_profiles.cause: Fluorescent compounds (polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons from combustion in ancient forest fires) producing strong blue fluorescence that dominates reflected light view.

color_profiles.name: Red / Cherry Amber
color_profiles.cause: Prolonged oxidation of surface or ancient oxidized material.

color_profiles.name: Burmese Amber (Burmite)
color_profiles.cause: Age (100+ Ma — oldest significant amber) and specific resin chemistry of ancient Araucarian trees.

minerals.visual_notes: Amber's most famous feature is inclusion preservation — ancient insects, spiders, plant fragments, feathers, and even vertebrate hair and skin have been preserved in extraordinary detail. The warm resinous golden color and organic warmth are unmatched by any mineral. Dominican blue amber appears an iridescent blue-green in reflected light — among the most extraordinary natural optical effects.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Significant fluorescence under UV — blue-white fluorescence common in Baltic amber; Dominican amber shows intense blue fluorescence (responsible for its blue color in daylight)
optical_effects.iridescence: Dominican blue amber shows strong blue-green iridescence from fluorescence; some amber shows interference colors in reflected light
optical_effects.play_of_color: Dominican blue amber shows strong blue-green iridescence from fluorescence; some amber shows interference colors in reflected light

color_profiles.name: Deep Siberian Amethyst
color_profiles.cause: High Fe4+ color center density; optimal iron content and irradiation history.

color_profiles.name: Standard Amethyst
color_profiles.cause: Fe4+ color centers at moderate concentration.

color_profiles.name: Rose de France Amethyst
color_profiles.cause: Low Fe4+ color center density.

color_profiles.name: Ametrine
color_profiles.cause: Temperature and oxidation state gradient during crystal growth produces amethyst (cooler zones) and citrine (warmer/more oxidizing zones) in one crystal.

minerals.visual_notes: Amethyst geodes from Brazil and Uruguay are among the most recognizable mineral display specimens globally. Hollow cavite filled with violet crystals pointing inward. The finest deep Siberian-grade material shows a red flash under incandescent light. Color zoning in angular patterns is characteristic and diagnostic.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Typically none to very weak; some pale specimens show weak bluish or greenish fluorescence

color_profiles.name: Santa Maria Aquamarine
color_profiles.cause: Higher Fe2+ content; optimum Fe2+/Fe3+ ratio for deepest blue.

color_profiles.name: Classic Aquamarine (Pale Blue)
color_profiles.cause: Fe2+ at moderate concentration.

color_profiles.name: Blue-Green Aquamarine
color_profiles.cause: Mixed Fe2+ (blue) and Fe3+ (yellowish) contributions.

color_profiles.name: Maxixe (Deep Blue — Unstable)
color_profiles.cause: Color centers from natural irradiation (not Fe); color fades in light

minerals.visual_notes: Aquamarine is the sea captured in hexagonal beryl — pale to deep blue with the clarity of tropical water. The Dom Pedro aquamarine (Smithsonian, carved by Bernd Munsteiner) is the largest faceted aquamarine in the world (10,363 ct). Long prismatic crystal form with striated faces is characteristic of large rough specimens.

optical_effects.fluorescence: None typically

color_profiles.name: Azurite Blue
color_profiles.cause: Cu2+ d-d electronic transition in distorted Jahn-Teller coordination; same chromophore mechanism as other blue copper minerals.

color_profiles.name: Azurmalachite
color_profiles.cause: Intimate intergrowth of azurite (blue) and malachite (green).

minerals.visual_notes: Among the most intensely colored blue minerals in existence. The deep azure color is immediately striking. Azurmalachite — the intergrowth with green malachite — produces some of the most visually dramatic natural patterns in mineralogy.

optical_effects.fluorescence: None typical

color_profiles.name: goshenite
color_profiles.cause: Absence of chromophore impurities; near-ideal Be3Al2Si6O18 composition.

color_profiles.name: emerald
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ and/or V3+ substituting for Al3+ in the octahedral site.

color_profiles.name: aquamarine
color_profiles.cause: Fe2+ in channel sites (and Fe2+→Fe3+ charge transfer) produces blue hues.

color_profiles.name: morganite
color_profiles.cause: Mn2+/Mn3+ substituting in the structure; sometimes Cs-enrichment.

color_profiles.name: heliodor
color_profiles.cause: Fe3+ in channel sites producing yellow; combined Fe2+/Fe3+ gives yellow-green.

color_profiles.name: red-beryl
color_profiles.cause: Mn3+ substituting for Al3+ in the octahedral site.

minerals.visual_notes: Hexagonal cross-section is diagnostic in hand specimens. Prismatic crystals can be enormous in pegmatites (multi-kilogram). Emerald is densely included compared to other varieties. Color zoning (core-to-rim or sector) occurs in some crystals.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Weak to none in most varieties; emerald may show weak red fluorescence under LW UV (Cr-bearing); synthetic emerald often stronger red
optical_effects.chatoyancy: Rare cat's-eye beryl known; requires parallel tube inclusions

color_profiles.name: Colorless / Iceland Spar
color_profiles.cause: Absence of chromophore impurities; pure CaCO3.

color_profiles.name: White / Chalk / Limestone
color_profiles.cause: Microcrystalline or fine-grained aggregate; scattering of light.

color_profiles.name: Mangano-Calcite
color_profiles.cause: Mn2+ substituting for Ca2+ in the octahedral site.

color_profiles.name: Orange / Amber Calcite
color_profiles.cause: Iron oxides and organic matter inclusions.

color_profiles.name: Green Calcite
color_profiles.cause: Chlorite inclusions or trace impurities.

color_profiles.name: Blue Calcite
color_profiles.cause: Trace impurities or structural defects; exact mechanism varies.

color_profiles.name: Black Calcite
color_profiles.cause: Organic matter inclusions (bitumen).

minerals.visual_notes: Calcite's extraordinary morphological diversity makes it among the most recognizable minerals despite no single defining habit. Clear Iceland spar shows remarkable double refraction — images appear doubled. Dogtooth clusters are among the most architecturally beautiful mineral specimens.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Highly variable — many specimens fluoresce red, pink, orange, yellow, or blue under UV; mangano-calcite often fluoresces vivid pink-red under SW UV
optical_effects.iridescence: Rare iridescence from thin-film interference in some specimens
optical_effects.play_of_color: Rare iridescence from thin-film interference in some specimens
optical_effects.chatoyancy: Fibrous satin-spar calcite shows cat's eye effect

color_profiles.name: Blue Celestite
color_profiles.cause: Color centers from trace impurities and/or radiation effects; mechanism not fully established.

color_profiles.name: Colorless / White Celestite
color_profiles.cause: Absence of chromophore; pure SrSO4.

color_profiles.name: Orange / Yellow Celestite
color_profiles.cause: Iron oxide staining or trace impurities.

minerals.visual_notes: Madagascar celestite geodes — hollow limestone nodules lined with pale-blue tabular crystals — are among the most widely sold mineral specimens globally. The celestial blue color is gentle and consistent. Crystals often have a translucent quality with pearly luster on cleavage surfaces.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Some specimens fluoresce pale blue or white under SW UV
optical_effects.chatoyancy: Fibrous material may show weak cat's eye

color_profiles.name: chalcedony-blue
color_profiles.cause: Rayleigh scattering of light through the microporous fibrous structure.

color_profiles.name: carnelian
color_profiles.cause: Iron oxide (hematite/goethite) inclusions.

color_profiles.name: chrysoprase
color_profiles.cause: Nickel (as nickeloan talc or willemseite) imparting green.

color_profiles.name: onyx
color_profiles.cause: Parallel straight bands; black from carbon or manganese inclusions.

color_profiles.name: bloodstone
color_profiles.cause: Green from chlorite inclusions; red spots from iron oxide (hematite/jasper).

minerals.visual_notes: Characteristic waxy luster on polished surfaces. Translucent with a faint glow in thin sections. Smooth, ghost-like quality distinguishes it from coarse quartz.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Variable; some specimens weakly fluorescent under UV.
optical_effects.chatoyancy: Weak; fibrous varieties can show faint cat's eye in cabochon cut.

color_profiles.name: cyan-chrysocolla
color_profiles.cause: Cu2+ in the structure; high Cu content with low Fe.

color_profiles.name: blue-green-chrysocolla
color_profiles.cause: Cu2+ with minor Fe or Mn shifting hue.

color_profiles.name: gem-silica
color_profiles.cause: Cu2+ in chalcedony host; silicification increases hardness and translucency.

color_profiles.name: green-chrysocolla
color_profiles.cause: High Cu with slight oxidation state shift; mixing with malachite.

minerals.visual_notes: The vivid cyan to turquoise-blue color is unmistakable. Chrysocolla is often the most visually striking mineral in copper oxidation zones. It frequently occurs with malachite (green), azurite (deep blue), and cuprite (red), creating spectacular polychrome specimens.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Generally inert

color_profiles.name: Pale Yellow Citrine
color_profiles.cause: Fe3+ at low concentration; may be natural.

color_profiles.name: Golden Citrine
color_profiles.cause: Fe3+ at moderate concentration; most heat-treated amethyst.

color_profiles.name: Madeira Citrine
color_profiles.cause: Elevated Fe3+ at high temperature; always heat-treated (amethyst originally).

color_profiles.name: Ametrine
color_profiles.cause: Natural zoning in crystal growth; amethyst zones (Fe4+) and citrine zones (Fe3+) coexist.

minerals.visual_notes: Citrine spans from pale lemon-yellow to deep Madeira orange-cognac. The warm golden tones made it popular in Art Deco jewelry. Large clean specimens are readily available and affordable. Heat-treated material has uniform color; natural citrine often has patchy or pale color with visible phantoms.

optical_effects.fluorescence: None typically

color_profiles.name: Fresh Native Copper
color_profiles.cause: metallic copper surface — characteristic d-band optical absorption

color_profiles.name: Oxidized / Tarnished
color_profiles.cause: formation of cuprite (Cu2O) or tenorite surface layer

color_profiles.name: Patinated / Weathered
color_profiles.cause: formation of malachite, azurite, chrysocolla, or brochantite on surface

minerals.visual_notes: Unmistakable copper-red when fresh. Slow oxidation spectrum from red through brown and black to vivid blue-green patina is one of nature's most beautiful chemical sequences. Keweenaw Peninsula specimens show exceptional arborescent habits.

optical_effects.fluorescence: none

color_profiles.name: colorless
color_profiles.cause: Absence of chromophore trace elements.

color_profiles.name: ruby
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ substituting for Al3+; d-d electronic transitions.

color_profiles.name: blue-sapphire
color_profiles.cause: Intervalence charge transfer between Fe2+ and Ti4+ on adjacent octahedral sites.

color_profiles.name: yellow-sapphire
color_profiles.cause: Fe3+ or color centers.

color_profiles.name: padparadscha
color_profiles.cause: Combined Cr3+ and iron-related centers.

color_profiles.name: pink-sapphire
color_profiles.cause: Low Cr3+ concentrations; boundary with ruby is debated.

minerals.visual_notes: Gem-quality crystals prized as ruby and sapphire. Non-gem material is gray-brown and often massive. Silk inclusions produce asterism in star rubies and sapphires.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Variable; rubies often fluoresce strong red under UV; blue sapphires typically inert.
optical_effects.chatoyancy: Present in star corundums when cut en cabochon.
optical_effects.asterism: Six-rayed stars in star ruby and star sapphire from intersecting rutile needle (silk) inclusions along {0001}.

color_profiles.name: Colorless (D–F on GIA scale)
color_profiles.cause: structural purity; absence of nitrogen and other chromophores

color_profiles.name: Cape Yellow / Fancy Yellow
color_profiles.cause: nitrogen impurities substituting for carbon (Type Ia / Type Ib)

color_profiles.name: Brown Diamond
color_profiles.cause: plastic deformation and vacancy clusters along crystal planes

color_profiles.name: Blue Diamond
color_profiles.cause: boron impurities (Type IIb)

color_profiles.name: Pink / Red Diamond
color_profiles.variety_name: Argyle Pink, Fancy Pink, Red Diamond
color_profiles.cause: structural defects / plastic deformation (precise mechanism still debated)

color_profiles.name: Green Diamond
color_profiles.cause: natural radiation exposure (alpha particle irradiation of crystal lattice)

color_profiles.name: Black Diamond (Carbonado)
color_profiles.cause: polycrystalline aggregate with inclusions of graphite, amorphous carbon, and sulfides

textures: smooth adamantine luster on fresh crystal faces
frosted or etched on alluvial grains
trigon etch pits on octahedral faces
minerals.visual_notes: Unmistakable adamantine luster and exceptional brilliance in cut stones. Rough diamonds often appear greasy or frosted. Dispersion produces fire — flashes of spectral color — unmatched by any other colorless gemstone except moissanite.

optical_effects.fluorescence: blue fluorescence common (~25–35% of gem diamonds) under shortwave UV; some fluoresce yellow, orange, or green; many inert

color_profiles.name: Colombian Emerald
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ dominant chromophore; some V3+. Low Fe keeps color pure without brownish or yellowish cast.

color_profiles.name: Zambian Emerald
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ + some Fe2+; slightly deeper/darker than Colombian.

color_profiles.name: Brazilian Emerald
color_profiles.cause: V3+ dominant in some deposits; Fe adds yellowish-green component.

color_profiles.name: Zimbabwe Emerald
color_profiles.cause: High Cr3+ in very small stones from mica schist.

minerals.visual_notes: Emerald's vivid green is the benchmark for lush saturated green in the gem world. The jardin (inclusion garden) is not a flaw in fine emerald — it is expected, accepted, and sometimes considered a signature of natural origin. The 'emerald cut' (rectangular step cut) was designed to showcase emerald's color while minimizing mechanical stress on the included stone.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Weak to moderate red fluorescence under LW UV (Cr3+); varies by origin — Colombian often weakly red, synthetic usually stronger red, Fe-rich specimens often inert

color_profiles.name: Classic Fire Opal (Orange)
color_profiles.cause: Iron oxide inclusions (hematite/goethite) and structural/organic chromophores in amorphous silica.

color_profiles.name: Yellow Fire Opal
color_profiles.cause: Lower iron oxide content; other trace chromophores.

color_profiles.name: Red Fire Opal
color_profiles.cause: Higher iron oxide concentration.

color_profiles.name: Precious Fire Opal
color_profiles.cause: Combination of iron-oxide chromophore AND ordered silica sphere diffraction.

minerals.visual_notes: Fire opal's vivid orange-to-red body color makes it the only facetable opal variety (when transparent). The color evokes live flame, molten metal, and sunset. In Mexico it is called 'piedra de fuego' (stone of fire) and was sacred to the Aztecs as a symbol of Huitzilopochtli.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Some specimens fluoresce green or yellow-green under LW UV
optical_effects.iridescence: Present in precious fire opal; absent in common fire opal
optical_effects.play_of_color: Present in precious fire opal; absent in common fire opal

color_profiles.name: purple-spectrum
color_profiles.cause: Impurity / defect driven coloration.

color_profiles.name: green-spectrum
color_profiles.cause: Impurity / defect driven coloration.

color_profiles.name: warm-spectrum
color_profiles.cause: Impurity / defect driven coloration.

color_profiles.name: dark-spectrum
color_profiles.cause: Rare dark color expression.

minerals.visual_notes: One of the best minerals for geometric crystal logic and surreal color zoning.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Often notable; the term fluorescence is historically derived from fluorite.

color_profiles.name: Pure Gold
color_profiles.cause: inherent electronic structure of metallic gold

color_profiles.name: Electrum
color_profiles.cause: natural silver alloy diluting gold color

color_profiles.name: Copper-Rich Gold
color_profiles.cause: copper substitution in metallic lattice

minerals.visual_notes: Unmistakable warm golden-yellow in fresh specimens. Arborescent and dendritic forms are among the most prized mineral specimens globally.

optical_effects.fluorescence: none

color_profiles.name: Crystalline Graphite
color_profiles.cause: carbon layers with metallic-character electron delocalization

color_profiles.name: Massive / Amorphous Graphite
color_profiles.cause: fine-grained or poorly crystallized carbon aggregate

minerals.visual_notes: Unmistakable greasy feel and extremely dark matte-black color. Marks paper and skin readily. Steel-gray metallic luster on fresh cleavage faces. Often found as disseminated flakes or foliated masses in metamorphic rocks.

optical_effects.fluorescence: none

color_profiles.name: earthy-red
color_profiles.cause: Fine-grained hematite; surface scattering of light.

color_profiles.name: specular
color_profiles.cause: Large platy crystals with near-perfect basal cleavage planes reflecting light.

color_profiles.name: kidney-ore
color_profiles.cause: Botryoidal surface morphology; fine-grained interior.

color_profiles.name: iron-rose
color_profiles.cause: Penetration twin aggregates forming rosette shapes.

minerals.visual_notes: One of the most visually variable oxides. Specular hematite is mirror-silver; earthy hematite is brick-red. Red streak unites all forms. Iron roses are among the most dramatic mineral specimens.

optical_effects.fluorescence: None.
optical_effects.iridescence: Iridescent tarnish colors sometimes visible on specular plates.
optical_effects.play_of_color: Iridescent tarnish colors sometimes visible on specular plates.

color_profiles.name: imperial-jade
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ substituting for Al3+ in the M1 octahedral site, producing a chromium absorption spectrum analogous to emerald.

color_profiles.name: fei-cui-green
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ for vivid greens; Fe2+/Fe3+ for duller or bluish greens.

color_profiles.name: lavender-jadeite
color_profiles.cause: Mn2+ and/or Fe2+–Fe3+ charge transfer in the structure; exact mechanism debated.

color_profiles.name: white-jadeite
color_profiles.cause: Low chromophore content; fine-grained texture scatters light.

color_profiles.name: black-jadeite
color_profiles.cause: High Fe3+ (aegirine component) or graphite/carbonaceous inclusions.

minerals.visual_notes: Jadeite's waxy polish and semi-transparency in the finest green material ('glassy' or 'icy') is visually unmistakable. The fei-cui terminology in Chinese trade describes a full quality spectrum. A water-drop test (drops stand high on polished surface) and the cold, greasy feel are tactile identifiers used by experienced traders.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Inert (untreated) to weak white or pale green under LW UV; heavily treated (B-jade) may show blue-white fluorescence

color_profiles.name: red-jasper
color_profiles.cause: Hematite (iron oxide) inclusions distributed through silica matrix.

color_profiles.name: yellow-brown-jasper
color_profiles.cause: Goethite and limonite (hydrous iron oxides).

color_profiles.name: green-jasper
color_profiles.cause: Chlorite, actinolite, or iron silicate inclusions.

color_profiles.name: picture-jasper
color_profiles.cause: Differential impurity banding and inclusion patterns creating landscape-like imagery.

color_profiles.name: ocean-jasper
color_profiles.cause: Orbicular spherulitic structure in rhyolite-hosted chalcedony/jasper.

color_profiles.name: mookaite
color_profiles.cause: Radiolarite-origin silicite with varied iron and organic impurities.

minerals.visual_notes: Opaque throughout — this is the key visual identifier. Takes a good polish showing vivid matte-to-waxy colors. Picture jasper is among the most visually complex natural stone materials.

color_profiles.name: labradorescence-blue-green
color_profiles.cause: Thin-film interference from Ca-rich and Na-rich exsolution lamellae at ~100–300 nm spacing. Shorter lamellar spacing produces blue; longer spacing shifts toward green and gold.

color_profiles.name: labradorescence-gold-copper
color_profiles.cause: Longer-spacing exsolution lamellae shifting interference toward yellow-orange wavelengths.

color_profiles.name: spectrolite
color_profiles.cause: Full-range lamellar spacing distribution on a very dark ground mass, producing all visible colors.

color_profiles.name: transparent-gem
color_profiles.cause: Body color from iron or other trace impurities; transparent facetable material.

minerals.visual_notes: The labradorescence appears as a floating, depth-inhabiting iridescence that shifts with viewing angle and light direction. Unlike surface iridescence, it appears to come from within the stone. The dark gray to near-black base color intensifies the color display. Best specimens show full-spectrum color shift across a single face.

optical_effects.iridescence: Labradorescence: intense thin-film interference iridescence showing blue, green, gold, copper, orange — and in spectrolite, the full visible spectrum — from exsolution lamellae oriented parallel to {010}. The effect is directional and changes with both viewing angle and light angle.
optical_effects.play_of_color: Labradorescence: intense thin-film interference iridescence showing blue, green, gold, copper, orange — and in spectrolite, the full visible spectrum — from exsolution lamellae oriented parallel to {010}. The effect is directional and changes with both viewing angle and light angle.

color_profiles.name: lavender-lepidolite
color_profiles.cause: Mn2+ and/or Mn3+ substituting in octahedral sites; trace amounts produce pastel lavender.

color_profiles.name: pink-lepidolite
color_profiles.cause: Higher Mn content shifting from lavender toward pink.

color_profiles.name: purple-lepidolite
color_profiles.cause: Elevated Mn3+ content; sometimes enhanced by inclusions.

color_profiles.name: colorless-white-lepidolite
color_profiles.cause: Absence of Mn or other chromophores; high F content typical.

color_profiles.name: yellow-lepidolite
color_profiles.cause: Minor Fe3+ or specific structural defects.

minerals.visual_notes: Lepidolite is one of the most visually distinctive micas due to its lavender/pink color — the only common lavender mica. In pegmatites, it often forms spectacular masses of pale lavender scales intergrown with pink tourmaline (rubellite) and rose quartz. The pearly luster on cleavage surfaces gives a soft, shimmering quality.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Weak cream to yellow under SW UV in some specimens; generally weak
optical_effects.iridescence: Subtle adularescence-like pearly shimmer on cleavage surfaces
optical_effects.play_of_color: Subtle adularescence-like pearly shimmer on cleavage surfaces

color_profiles.name: classic-malachite-green
color_profiles.cause: Copper-bearing chemistry.

minerals.visual_notes: One of the strongest 'ornamental pattern' minerals in existence.

color_profiles.name: blue-moonstone
colors: colorless to white body; blue floating glow
color_profiles.cause: Thin albite exsolution lamellae (10–100 nm) produce Rayleigh/Tyndall scattering and constructive interference for blue wavelengths.

color_profiles.name: white-moonstone
colors: white body; white to milky-white floating glow
color_profiles.cause: Thicker lamellae scatter all visible wavelengths approximately equally, producing white adularescence.

color_profiles.name: peach-moonstone
colors: warm-beige body; peach to white glow
color_profiles.cause: Iron impurities impart warm body color; adularescence still present.

color_profiles.name: rainbow-moonstone
colors: colorless to white body; multicolor iridescence — blue
color_profiles.cause: Trade name for colorless to white labradorite showing adularescence-like labradorescence. Not orthoclase moonstone.

color_profiles.name: gray-moonstone
colors: gray body; blue to white floating glow
color_profiles.cause: Iron or other inclusions giving gray body color; adularescence retained.

color_profiles.name: cat-eye-moonstone
color_profiles.cause: Needle-like inclusions aligned with exsolution lamellae create chatoyancy in addition to adularescence.

minerals.visual_notes: Adularescence appears as a floating, glowing light phenomenon that rolls or shifts as the stone is moved — unlike surface iridescence, it appears to emanate from within the stone. The finest blue moonstone from Sri Lanka has an electric, almost liquid blue light that rolls across the interior.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Weak bluish or yellowish fluorescence in some specimens under longwave UV.
optical_effects.iridescence: Adularescence: the defining optical phenomenon. A floating, billowing blue or white light glow from alternating orthoclase/albite exsolution lamellae. Caused by Rayleigh/Tyndall scattering and constructive thin-film interference. Blue adularescence (from thin lamellae) is most prized. Effect is direction-dependent and orientation-critical in cutting.
optical_effects.play_of_color: Adularescence: the defining optical phenomenon. A floating, billowing blue or white light glow from alternating orthoclase/albite exsolution lamellae. Caused by Rayleigh/Tyndall scattering and constructive thin-film interference. Blue adularescence (from thin lamellae) is most prized. Effect is direction-dependent and orientation-critical in cutting.
optical_effects.chatoyancy: Rare; cat's eye moonstone exists with needle inclusions.

color_profiles.name: Rose / Pure Pink Morganite
color_profiles.cause: Mn2+/Mn3+ at moderate concentration; ideal ratio for purest rose-pink.

color_profiles.name: Peach Morganite
color_profiles.cause: Higher Mn with Fe contributions or natural irradiation color centers.

color_profiles.name: Deep Pink / Hot Pink Morganite
color_profiles.cause: Higher Mn3+ concentration.

minerals.visual_notes: Morganite's soft rose-peach color is inherently gentle and warm — less assertive than ruby, less cold than pink sapphire. Large clean crystals are available. The rose-gold + morganite combination became dominant in contemporary jewelry design in the 2010s.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Weak to none

color_profiles.name: spinach-jade
color_profiles.cause: Moderate to high Fe2+ substituting for Mg in actinolite-rich nephrite; Fe-rich composition.

color_profiles.name: mutton-fat-jade
color_profiles.cause: Low Fe content; near-pure tremolite composition; fine texture diffuses light.

color_profiles.name: celadon-jade
color_profiles.cause: Low Fe content with minor chromophores.

color_profiles.name: gray-nephrite
color_profiles.cause: Graphite inclusions or specific Fe oxidation states.

color_profiles.name: black-nephrite
color_profiles.cause: Very high Fe content and/or carbonaceous inclusions.

minerals.visual_notes: Nephrite's waxy sheen and subtle translucency are distinctive. White mutton-fat jade has a soft luminosity unlike any other stone. The green varieties can look similar to jadeite but typically show a more subdued, less intense green. The fibrous microstructure is visible under magnification as a tangled felt-like mass.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Generally inert; some material shows very weak cream or green fluorescence under LW UV

color_profiles.name: Classic Black Obsidian
color_profiles.cause: Iron and titanium oxides (magnetite) distributed throughout glass matrix.

color_profiles.name: Rainbow Obsidian
colors: black with rainbow iridescence bands — green
color_profiles.cause: Thin layers of oriented magnetite nanoparticles creating thin-film interference.

color_profiles.name: Snowflake Obsidian
color_profiles.cause: Devitrification spherulites of cristobalite (silica) and feldspar crystallizing post-emplacement.

color_profiles.name: Mahogany Obsidian
color_profiles.cause: Iron oxide-rich zones (hematite/goethite) banding through black glass.

color_profiles.name: Apache Tears
color_profiles.cause: Small rounded nodules of obsidian that weather from perlite matrix; translucent in thin section.

minerals.visual_notes: Obsidian is pure volcanic glass — the most visually simple and the most dramatically effective black mineral material. Its jet-black vitreous surface reflects light cleanly. Rainbow varieties produce full spectral color from thin-film magnetite layers. Apache tears appear opaque but transmit brownish light when held up.

optical_effects.fluorescence: None typically
optical_effects.iridescence: Rainbow obsidian — full spectral iridescence from nanolaminate magnetite interference layers
optical_effects.play_of_color: Rainbow obsidian — full spectral iridescence from nanolaminate magnetite interference layers
optical_effects.chatoyancy: Sheen obsidian — silver or gold sheen from parallel nanoparticle layers

color_profiles.name: White Precious Opal
color_profiles.cause: White to light-gray body color from common opal base; play-of-color from sphere diffraction.

color_profiles.name: Black Precious Opal
colors: dark-blue body with vivid spectral play-of-color
color_profiles.cause: Dark body color (from carbon or iron oxide traces) intensifies play-of-color contrast.

color_profiles.name: Crystal Opal
colors: transparent to semi-transparent with spectral fire
color_profiles.cause: Pure to near-pure silica spheres with no body color obscuring transparency.

color_profiles.name: Boulder Opal
colors: brown ironstone matrix with opal veins showing spectral color
color_profiles.cause: Thin seams of precious opal in ironstone host; cut with matrix.

color_profiles.name: Common Opal (Potch)
color_profiles.cause: Irregular or absent sphere arrangement; no diffraction grating.

minerals.visual_notes: Precious opal's play-of-color is unlike any other optical effect in the mineral world — full spectrum visible in shifting patches as the viewing angle changes. The diffraction grating created by ordered spheres splits white light into all visible wavelengths simultaneously.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Variable; many specimens fluoresce white, green, yellow, or blue-white under UV; some inert
optical_effects.iridescence: Play-of-color is a diffraction phenomenon (not thin-film iridescence); produces spectral flashes across the full visible range
optical_effects.play_of_color: Play-of-color is a diffraction phenomenon (not thin-film iridescence); produces spectral flashes across the full visible range

color_profiles.name: red-orange-petrified-wood
color_profiles.cause: Fe2O3 (hematite) in the replacing chalcedony; oxidizing conditions during silicification.

color_profiles.name: yellow-brown-petrified-wood
color_profiles.cause: FeO(OH) goethite/limonite; partially hydrated iron oxides.

color_profiles.name: black-petrified-wood
color_profiles.cause: MnO2 (pyrolusite) or carbonaceous material preserved in low-oxygen conditions.

color_profiles.name: gray-petrified-wood
color_profiles.cause: Low trace element content in pure silica replacement; minimal chromophores.

color_profiles.name: blue-green-petrified-wood
color_profiles.cause: Cu or Cr in the replacing silica.

color_profiles.name: agatized-wood
color_profiles.cause: Multiple silica precipitation events with varying trace element concentrations, producing banding analogous to agate.

minerals.visual_notes: The preserved wood grain and annual rings are the defining visual feature — the stone tells a biological story. Cross-sections are among the most visually striking mineral-art objects, showing perfect tree anatomy in glowing chalcedony. Color zonation often follows the original wood structure.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Variable; some material fluoresces green or yellow under UV (chalcedony component)
optical_effects.chatoyancy: Rare; some fibrous chalcedony replacements show weak chatoyancy

color_profiles.name: Classic Brassy Pyrite
color_profiles.cause: Metallic luster from d-band electronic structure of iron sulfide; brass-yellow reflectance.

color_profiles.name: Iridescent Tarnished Pyrite
color_profiles.cause: Thin-film iron oxide/hydroxide layer from surface oxidation (FeS2 → Fe oxide + H2SO4).

color_profiles.name: Pyrite Sun / Dollar Pyrite
colors: flat disc with radiating crystal striations
color_profiles.cause: Radial crystal growth in confined space (coal seam cleavage planes).

minerals.visual_notes: Pyrite is mineralogy's great impersonator — 'fool's gold' for millennia. The striated faces of pyrite cubes (from oscillation between cube and pyritohedron) are diagnostic. Iron cross twins — two interlocking pyritohedra — are among mineralogy's most architecturally perfect specimens. Pyrite suns (flat radiating discs) are unique and immediately recognizable forms.

optical_effects.fluorescence: None

color_profiles.name: rock-crystal
color_profiles.cause: Low impurity content / near-ideal composition.

color_profiles.name: smoky
color_profiles.cause: Color centers associated with irradiation and trace impurities.

color_profiles.name: rose
color_profiles.cause: Variety-specific color mechanism; often treated as inclusion/defect related.

color_profiles.name: amethyst-spectrum
color_profiles.cause: Impurity / defect-driven color.

minerals.visual_notes: One of the most visually flexible minerals on earth.

color_profiles.name: Raspberry / Crystal Rhodochrosite
color_profiles.cause: High Mn content with minimal Fe/Ca dilution; Mn2+ d-d transition.

color_profiles.name: Inca Rose (Banded Stalactitic)
color_profiles.cause: Alternating layers with varying Mn/Ca/Fe ratios; banding reflects changing fluid chemistry.

color_profiles.name: Orange-Brown Rhodochrosite
color_profiles.cause: Higher Fe substitution (toward siderite) diluting and warming the pink.

minerals.visual_notes: The banded 'Inca Rose' stalactitic form from Argentina — polished to reveal concentric pink-and-white rings — is one of the most recognized ornamental stone patterns in the gem world. Crystallized raspberry-red specimens from Colorado are among the most sought-after collector mineral specimens globally.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Weak; some specimens show pale pink or orange fluorescence under UV

color_profiles.name: Classic Rose Quartz
color_profiles.cause: Microscopic pink fibrous inclusion of Ti-bearing Al-phosphate hydroxide mineral distributed through massive quartz; optical scattering.

color_profiles.name: Star Rose Quartz
color_profiles.cause: Rutile needle inclusions in three orientations within quartz produce asterism when cut as cabochon.

color_profiles.name: Deep Rose / Intense Pink
color_profiles.cause: Higher concentration of color-bearing inclusions.

minerals.visual_notes: Rose quartz has a characteristic soft, diffuse, milky-pink quality from the light-scattering fibrous inclusions. It is never facet-grade transparent in classic form. The color is gentle and warm — not vivid like ruby or pink tourmaline but consistently soft. Star rose quartz cut as cabochon shows the six-rayed star most vividly under a point light source.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Weak blue or orange-pink under UV
optical_effects.asterism: Star rose quartz — six-rayed star from oriented rutile inclusions; best shown in Sri Lankan and Brazilian material

color_profiles.name: Pigeon's Blood Ruby
colors: pure-red with slight blue fluorescent overtone
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ at optimal concentration (~0.5–1.5% Cr2O3); strong red fluorescence under UV complements natural redness in daylight.

color_profiles.name: Burmese Ruby (general)
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ chromophore; trace Fe adds depth.

color_profiles.name: Thai/Cambodian Ruby
color_profiles.cause: Higher Fe content alongside Cr3+ creates darker, less fluorescent red.

color_profiles.name: Star Ruby
colors: red body with six-rayed white asterism
color_profiles.cause: Silk inclusions (rutile needles oriented along three crystallographic directions) producing asterism when cut as cabochon.

minerals.visual_notes: Ruby is the red variety of corundum — the intensity of Cr3+ fluorescence in daylight gives Burmese rubies an inner glow that red garnets and spinel cannot replicate. In strong sunlight, a fine Burmese ruby appears to emit light. The six-rayed star in star ruby is one of the most captivating optical effects in gemology.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Strong red fluorescence under UV (LW and SW) — Cr3+ emission; Burmese/marble-hosted rubies fluoresce more strongly than basalt-hosted Thai rubies (which have higher Fe quenching fluorescence)
optical_effects.asterism: Six-rayed star in star ruby — from intersecting rutile (silk) needle inclusions parallel to three directions in {0001} basal plane

color_profiles.name: Kashmir Blue Sapphire
color_profiles.cause: Fe2+/Ti4+ IVCT; fine silk inclusions scatter light to produce the famous velvety texture.

color_profiles.name: Burmese / Ceylon Sapphire
color_profiles.cause: Fe2+/Ti4+ charge transfer — less silk than Kashmir; brighter.

color_profiles.name: Padparadscha
color_profiles.cause: Combination of Cr3+ (pink) and iron-related (orange) color centers.

color_profiles.name: Yellow Sapphire
color_profiles.cause: Fe3+ substitution or color centers.

color_profiles.name: Star Sapphire
colors: black body with six-rayed white asterism
color_profiles.cause: Rutile (silk) needle inclusions in three crystallographic orientations producing asterism under cabochon cut.

color_profiles.name: Parti Sapphire
color_profiles.cause: Zoned distribution of different chromophores (Fe, Ti) during crystal growth.

minerals.visual_notes: Kashmir sapphire produces a 'velvety' blue that seems to glow from within — caused by fine silk inclusions scattering light. Star sapphire at its best shows a perfectly centered six-rayed star that moves over the cabochon surface. Padparadscha's salmon-pink-orange is unique and defies category — it must be evaluated in person to appreciate.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Blue sapphires typically inert to faint under UV; some Sri Lankan sapphires show orange fluorescence; yellow sapphires may fluoresce orange
optical_effects.asterism: Six-rayed star in star sapphire from three sets of rutile needles in basal plane

color_profiles.name: Fresh Native Silver
color_profiles.cause: reflective metallic surface of unoxidized silver

color_profiles.name: Tarnished Silver
color_profiles.cause: silver sulfide (acanthite) tarnish layer from H2S exposure

minerals.visual_notes: Fresh specimens show brilliant silver-white metallic luster. Tarnish develops complex gray-black patinas. Kongsberg wire-silver specimens are among the most celebrated native metal mineral specimens in existence.

optical_effects.fluorescence: none
optical_effects.iridescence: tarnish iridescence common on oxidized surfaces
optical_effects.play_of_color: tarnish iridescence common on oxidized surfaces

color_profiles.name: Cobaltian Smithsonite (Blue / Lavender)
color_profiles.cause: Co2+ substituting for Zn2+.

color_profiles.name: Cuprian Smithsonite (Green / Teal)
color_profiles.cause: Cu2+ substituting for Zn2+.

color_profiles.name: Manganian Smithsonite (Pink)
color_profiles.cause: Mn2+ substituting for Zn2+.

color_profiles.name: White / Gray Smithsonite
color_profiles.cause: Near-pure ZnCO3 with minimal chromophore substitution.

color_profiles.name: Yellow / Brown Smithsonite
color_profiles.cause: Fe2+/Fe3+ substitution or iron oxide staining.

minerals.visual_notes: Smithsonite is primarily known as a secondary zinc carbonate with exceptional color range and unusual pearlescent botryoidal habit. Cobalt-bearing blue smithsonite from Tsumeb and copper-bearing green from Arizona are collector favorites. The pearly luster of botryoidal forms is distinctive.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Some specimens fluoresce pale blue, green, or white under UV

color_profiles.name: Classic Smoky Quartz
color_profiles.cause: Al-hole color centers from moderate irradiation dose.

color_profiles.name: Cairngorm
color_profiles.cause: Al-hole color centers with slight Fe contribution; lower irradiation dose.

color_profiles.name: Morion
color_profiles.cause: Very high irradiation dose; very dense color center population.

minerals.visual_notes: Smoky quartz produces the most varied depth range of any colored quartz — from barely-there smoke-gray to absolute black. Large faceted smoky quartz gems (hundreds to thousands of carats) are obtainable and affordable. The phantoms (ghost crystal growth zones) visible inside larger crystals add visual complexity.

optical_effects.fluorescence: None typically

color_profiles.name: Volcanic Fumarolic Sulfur
color_profiles.cause: sublimation deposits with minor impurities from volcanic gases

color_profiles.name: Sedimentary / Biogenic Sulfur
color_profiles.cause: bacterial reduction of sulfates (Desulfovibrio spp.)

color_profiles.name: Orange-Red Variety
color_profiles.cause: arsenic or selenium impurities

textures: earthy to powdery in massive form
minerals.visual_notes: Vivid sulfur-yellow color is highly diagnostic. Fine bipyramidal crystals from Sicily are among the most visually distinctive native element specimens. Surface luster characteristically resinous to waxy.

optical_effects.fluorescence: weak yellow-white under UV (some specimens)

color_profiles.name: oregon-sunstone-copper
color_profiles.cause: Native copper inclusions (platelets and dispersed copper) provide both color saturation and metallic aventurescent schiller.

color_profiles.name: norway-sunstone
color_profiles.cause: Hematite platelet inclusions oriented parallel to {001} in oligoclase matrix.

color_profiles.name: india-sunstone
color_profiles.cause: Goethite and hematite platelet inclusions in oligoclase.

color_profiles.name: colorless-clear-oregon
color_profiles.cause: Very low copper content; body color near-colorless.

minerals.visual_notes: Aventurescence in sunstone appears as a glittering, spangling metallic flash — brighter and more dramatic than the soft glow of adularescence in moonstone. Oregon sunstone is the only known feldspar with native copper inclusions, giving it a uniquely warm, saturated color. The metallic sparkle in Norwegian sunstone has a coppery-gold quality.

optical_effects.iridescence: Aventurescence: metallic glitter and schiller from oriented hematite, goethite, or native copper platelets. More dramatic than moonstone adularescence — appears as individual flashing points or a broad metallic sheen depending on inclusion density and size.
optical_effects.play_of_color: Aventurescence: metallic glitter and schiller from oriented hematite, goethite, or native copper platelets. More dramatic than moonstone adularescence — appears as individual flashing points or a broad metallic sheen depending on inclusion density and size.
optical_effects.asterism: Rare 4-ray star sunstone reported in Oregon material.

color_profiles.name: tiger-eye-classic
color_profiles.cause: Goethite (iron oxyhydroxide) inclusions within parallel silica fibers, replacing oxidized crocidolite.

color_profiles.name: hawk-eye
color_profiles.cause: Partial or unoxidized crocidolite fibers preserved or less fully replaced; blue color from original riebeckite amphibole.

color_profiles.name: ox-eye
color_profiles.cause: Further oxidation of goethite to hematite shifts color toward red-brown.

color_profiles.name: pietersite
color_profiles.cause: Brecciated combination of tiger's eye, hawk's eye, and jasper fragments.

minerals.visual_notes: Chatoyancy (cat's eye effect) is the defining visual feature. The golden-brown silky shimmer moves across the surface as the viewing angle shifts. The parallel fiber orientation is always visible in cross-section.

optical_effects.chatoyancy: Strong; classic golden-brown cat's eye effect from parallel goethite/iron oxide fibers within the silica matrix. Effect is best in cabochon cut.

color_profiles.name: schorl
color_profiles.cause: High Fe2+ and Fe3+ content; Fe2+–Fe3+ intervalence charge transfer.

color_profiles.name: rubellite
color_profiles.cause: Mn2+ and Mn3+ in the Y-site of elbaite.

color_profiles.name: indicolite
color_profiles.cause: Fe2+ and Fe3+ in elbaite (Li-Al-rich end member) producing blue hues.

color_profiles.name: verdelite
color_profiles.cause: Fe2+ and mixed Fe2+/Fe3+ in elbaite; Cr3+ in chrome tourmaline (dravite/uvite).

color_profiles.name: paraiba-tourmaline
color_profiles.cause: Cu2+ (and Mn for violet tones) in the Y-site; extremely rare Cu-bearing elbaite.

color_profiles.name: watermelon-tourmaline
color_profiles.cause: Growth zoning: Mn-rich (pink) core crystallized before Fe-richer (green) outer zones.

color_profiles.name: dravite
color_profiles.cause: Mg-dominant Y-site composition; Fe minor.

color_profiles.name: colorless-achroite
color_profiles.cause: Very low Fe, Mn, and other chromophores in elbaite.

minerals.visual_notes: The striated prism faces running parallel to the long axis (c-axis) are diagnostic in hand specimens. Cross-sections showing rounded triangular outline are highly characteristic. Watermelon tourmaline slices are immediately recognizable. Schorl is ubiquitous as jet-black prismatic crystals in pegmatites globally.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Generally inert to weak; some pink/red elbaite may show weak orange or red under LW UV
optical_effects.chatoyancy: Cat's-eye tourmaline known; parallel tube inclusions or growth channels along c-axis

color_profiles.name: Robin's Egg Blue
color_profiles.cause: High Cu2+ content; minimal Fe substitution for Al.

color_profiles.name: Matrix Turquoise
color_profiles.cause: Host rock (limonite, quartz, pyrite, iron oxides) veining through turquoise mass.

color_profiles.name: Green Turquoise
color_profiles.cause: Higher Fe3+ substituting for Al3+; or Zn or V substitution.

color_profiles.name: Blue-Green Turquoise
color_profiles.cause: Mixed Cu2+ and Fe3+ contributions.

minerals.visual_notes: Turquoise is one of the most culturally loaded minerals in human history. The blue that defined a color name ('turquoise'), ancient trade routes, and sacred objects across the Old and New Worlds. Matrix spiderweb patterns are prized for their aesthetic complexity — black or brown veins threading through blue-green backgrounds.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Typically none to faint; some specimens show weak blue-green or yellow under LW UV
optical_effects.chatoyancy: Rare; fibrous material can show weak cat's eye
